data.content_ref: inline:The mechanism claims ironworking 'democratized military capability' and eliminated the material basis for palatial redistribution, but this requires iron technology to have diffused widely enough to arm significant non-palatial forces before or during the collapse period (1200-1150 BC). Archaeometallurgical evidence shows iron remained rare and primarily ceremonial in this period; the 'men of iron' Hittite text (KUB XXXI 4) dates to the 13th century and refers to a specific status group, not widespread iron weaponry. The mechanism compresses a gradual technological transition (iron becoming dominant c. 900-700 BC) into a sudden causal trigger for 1200 BC collapse. Moreover, if iron's 'military inferiority against Egyptian composite bow/chariot tactics delayed transition' in Egypt, the same inferiority should have preserved bronze-based palace systems elsewhere—yet the claim says iron democratization caused collapse. This creates a selective asymmetry: iron's inferiority preserves Egypt but destroys others, without explaining why non-Egyptian elites couldn't similarly exploit bronze's tactical advantages against iron-armed opponents.
